title: Complement system & complement deficiencies
body_html:
Complement is a group of around 20 liver synthesised proteins. They are a vital component of the immune system. There are three types of triggers, or activation pathways, which can initiate the complement cascade where complement proteins sequentially activate other complement proteins.

Complement triggers the following immune functions:
  • Membrane attack complex: causes pathogen cell death via rupturing the cell wall.
  • Enhances phagocytosis via opsonization: C3b being the most important opsonin.
  • Inflammation: attracting macrophages and neutrophils.

Diagram illustrating the complement system. Note the final step of the compliment cascade is sometimes referred to as the lytic pathway. This is the part of the complement system that causes the death of bacterial cells. It's triggered by the splitting of C5 and the attachment of C5b to a target. Click on image to enlarge

Membrane attack complex (MAC)
Membrane attack complex (MAC) is formed by the sequential assembly of complement proteins C5b, C6, C7, C8, and multiple C9 molecules, which create a pore-like structure in the membrane of a target cell, leading to cell lysis and death.

Membrane attack complex (MAC) forming pore on pathogen cell membrane. Click on image to enlarge

C3b
As can be seen all activation pathways eventually lead to the splitting of C3 into C3a and C3b. C3b is an important complement protein that plays a role in:
  • Opsonisation
  • C3b may combine with other complement proteins to form C5 convertase and convert C5 to C5a and C5b. C5b recruits C6, C7, C8 and C9 to form membrane attack complex

Anaphylatoxins
Anaphylatoxins (C3a, C4a, C5a) are complement fragments that cause anaphylaxis-like reactions by:
  • Stimulating mast cell and basophil degranulation
  • Causing smooth muscle contraction
  • Increasing vascular permeability
  • Acting as chemoattractants (especially C5a)

Chemoattractants
C5a is the most potent chemoattractant among complement proteins.

C3a also has chemotactic properties but is much weaker than C5a.

Complement deficiency
Complement deficiencies may be congenital or acquired. Different complement deficiencies can have different outcomes. For example C1 deficiency can result in SLE as the body's ability to effectively clear away immune complexes and cellular debris is impaired, leading to an accumulation of autoantigens that can trigger an autoimmune response characteristic of SLE.

C3b and C5-C9 are involved in opsonisation and destruction of bacteria so deficiencies here lead to severe bacterial infection especially with encapsulated bacterial.

Complement control protein deficiencies
Conversely a deficiency in the enzymes that regulate complement can lead to various problems. For example deficiency of the enzyme that inhibit C1 acitivity (C1 esterase inhibitor) can result in hereditary angioedema (HAE) as the response to antigens is unregulated.

Complement decay-accelerating factor (DAF/CD55) is a membrane-bound regulatory protein that protects host cells from their own complement system by accelerating the breakdown of C3 and C5 convertases. Its deficiency, as seen in paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria (PNH), leads to complement-mediated destruction of the body's own cells, particularly red blood cells.

The table below summarises the deficiencies and associated diseases:

Complement DeficiencyInheritance PatternAssociated Clinical FeaturesIncreased Susceptibility to Infections
C1Autosomal recessiveSystemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)Not specifically noted
C2Autosomal recessiveSLE, recurrent pyogenic infections with encapsulated bacteria (especially pneumococcal), other autoimmune disorders (e.g., glomerulonephritis, vasculitis)Streptococcus pneumoniae
C3Autosomal recessiveRecurrent pyogenic infections with encapsulated bacteria from birth, glomerulonephritis, other immune complex disordersStreptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis
C4Autosomal recessiveSLE, other autoimmune disorders (e.g., IgA nephropathy, systemic sclerosis, type 1 diabetes mellitus)Not specifically noted
C5-C9 (Terminal Pathway Components)Autosomal recessiveRecurrent Neisseria meningitidis and disseminated Neisseria gonorrhoeae infectionsNeisseria meningitidis
Mannose-Binding Lectin (MBL)Autosomal recessiveRecurrent pyogenic infections with encapsulated bacteria from birth; unexplained sepsis; increased infection severity in secondary immunodeficienciesStreptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis
MASP-2UnknownAutoimmune disorders (e.g., inflammatory bowel disease, erythema multiforme), recurrent pyogenic infections with encapsulated bacteriaStreptococcus pneumoniae
Factor BAutosomal recessivePyogenic infectionsNot specifically noted
Factor DAutosomalPyogenic infectionsNot specifically noted
ProperdinX-linkedIncreased risk of fulminantNeisseria infection
C1 InhibitorAutosomal dominantAngioedemaNot specifically noted
DAF		AcquiredParoxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuriaNot specifically noted
Factor IAutosomal codominantSimilar to C3 deficiencyStreptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis
Factor HAutosomal codominantSimilar to C3 deficiency; hemolytic-uremic syndromeStreptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis

Deficiencies in components like C3, properdin, and terminal pathway components (C5-C9) are associated with increased susceptibility to infections by Neisseria meningitidis and Streptococcus pneumoniae.
